did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning, specifically the Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) classifier, to perform segmentation and classification of hyperspectral images for delineating tumor margins in basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC). The method involved segmenting hyperspectral images into regions with similar spectral and spatial features and then classifying these regions to generate classification maps of tumor margins.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'SCIENTIFIC PAPER' and a 'preliminary study' that presents original experimental work. It describes the acquisition of hyperspectral images from 11 histologically diagnosed carcinomas, the development of a new segmentation and machine learning-based classification method (using the Spectral Angle Mapper classifier), and the reporting of new results including AUC metrics for tumor margin delineation. It is not a review of prior literature but an original diagnostic accuracy and technical study.

title: Hyperspectral imaging with machine learning for in vivo skin carcinoma margin assessment: a preliminary study
journal: Physical and Engineering Sciences in Medicine
brief_summary: This preliminary study evaluated the use of hyperspectral imaging combined with machine learning for in vivo delineation of tumor margins in basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC). The researchers acquired hyperspectral images from 11 histologically diagnosed carcinomas and applied a segmentation process to identify regions with similar spectral and spatial features. These segments were classified using the Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) algorithm, with approximately 37% of the data used for training. The method's performance was assessed using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves and area under the curve (AUC) metrics. The results demonstrated very good performance, with median AUC values of 0.8924 for BCCs, 0.8014 for SCCs, and 0.8930 for normal skin. The study concludes that hyperspectral imaging with this machine learning approach can serve as an objective aid for preoperative evaluation of carcinoma margins.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in 6 patients (6 histologically diagnosed cases). Used as a target class for margin delineation and classification against normal skin.

Studied in 5 patients (5 histologically diagnosed cases). Used as a target class for margin delineation and classification against normal skin. Note: Bowen's disease is listed as a histopathological type for one patient (Patient 9) in Table 1, but the study groups it under the broader SCC category for analysis.
Used as the negative class/comparator for tumor margin delineation and classification. Classified alongside tumor types to generate classification maps.

all_task_types.target: Tissue class labels (tumor vs. normal skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a machine learning-based data classification process using the Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) classifier to distinguish between two classes: tumor (BCC or SCC) and normal skin. The method generates classification maps illustrating tumor margins by assigning each segmented region to one of the two discrete classes based on spectral similarity. The performance was evaluated using ROC curves and AUC metrics for binary discrimination between tissue types.

datasets_used.name: In vivo hyperspectral imaging cohort of skin carcinomas
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset consisting of hyperspectral images acquired from 11 patients (8 women, 3 men) with histologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma (BCC) or squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the face or scalp. Images were acquired using an ImSpector V8E system (Specim, Oulu, Finland) coupled with a DX4 camera before surgical excision. The dataset includes 205 spectral bands in the 400–800 nm range at a spatial resolution of 348 × 260 pixels.

use.training: Approximately 37% of the segments from each hyperspectral image were selected as reference (training) samples for the Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) classifier, based on Van Niel’s rule and physician recommendations.
use.testing: The remaining segments (approximately 63%) were used as unknown (testing) segments to evaluate the classification performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the proposed TMD method in distinguishing between tumor and normal skin tissue classes by plotting true positive rate against false positive rate at various threshold settings.
To quantify the capability of the method to differentiate tissue types; median, minimum, and maximum AUC values were calculated to assess overall performance.
To compare the differences between measured distances in dermoscopic images and calculated distances in the TMD classification maps, as the dermoscopic method was not considered a reference standard.
To describe the distribution and variability of AUC values for different tissue types (e.g., SCC and BCC) in box plots.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics, and agreement was assessed using Bland-Altman analysis.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.input_size: 205 spectral bands in the 400–800 nm range at a spatial resolution of 348 × 260 pixels

architecture_details.segmentation_method: Edge-based segmentation algorithm (ENVI v.5.1) followed by full lambda-schedule algorithm (Robinson et al.) to merge adjacent segments based on spectral and spatial features
architecture_details.classification_logic: Calculates the spectral angle between reference spectra (r) and unknown segment spectra (t) in a space with dimensionality equal to the number of bands (n). Segments are classified based on spectral similarity to reference samples.
architecture_details.threshold: Default maximum angle threshold of 0.1 radians

training_details.training_data_proportion: Approximately 37% of segments selected as reference (training) samples
training_details.training_selection_method: Van Niel’s rule and physician recommendations
training_details.testing_data_proportion: Approximately 63% of segments used as unknown (testing) segments

preprocessing_techniques: Calibration of hyperspectral images using dark reference (Dref) and white reference (Wref) images to convert digital numbers to reflectance
Manual selection of a region of interest (ROI) containing only the tumor area and surrounding normal skin to remove non-informative background
Edge-based segmentation algorithm to extract boundaries and create regions based on rapid changes in average pixel intensity
Full lambda-schedule algorithm to merge adjacent segments based on spectral and spatial features (scale level: 30, merge level: 75)
Calculation of mean pixel values for each segment to represent the segment's spectral signature
conclusions: The proposed method, which combines hyperspectral image segmentation with the Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) machine learning classifier, demonstrated very good performance in delineating tumor margins for basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC).

The method achieved median Area Under the Curve (AUC) values of 0.8924 for BCCs, 0.8014 for SCCs, and 0.8930 for normal skin, with AUC values above 0.89 for all tissue types considered as very good performance.

The TMD method consistently achieved high AUC values for SCCs, likely due to the homogeneity of the SCC group in terms of histopathological grading, while some BCC margins were identified better than SCCs, possibly due to the clearer borders of nodular BCCs.

The method proved most efficient in classifying normal skin, showing a relatively uniform distribution of AUC values around the median (0.8930), indicating that normal skin is spectrally more homogenous than tumors regardless of skin phenotype.

Visual diagnosis of tumor margins using standard imaging is practically impossible due to spectral similarities in shape but differences in intensity; however, the spectral differences in intensity allowed for clear differentiation between tumor and normal skin classes.

A comparative analysis using Bland-Altman plots showed a mean difference of 0.58 mm between distances measured by the TMD method and those measured via dermoscopy, with the line of equality lying within the confidence interval of the mean difference.
Hyperspectral imaging combined with this machine learning approach can serve as an objective aid for the preoperative evaluation of carcinoma margins.
important_remarks: The study is explicitly labeled as a 'preliminary study' due to the very small sample size (n=11 patients: 6 BCC, 5 SCC), which limits statistical power and generalizability.

There is significant heterogeneity in histological subtypes within the BCC group (e.g., nodular, infiltrative, superficial), whereas the SCC group was more homogeneous in grading, potentially biasing the comparison of margin delineation difficulty.

The study lacks validation against the gold standard of histopathological examination; instead, it compares results with dermoscopy, which the authors note is not a reference standard, and acknowledges that tissue shrinkage during surgery makes direct histological correlation difficult.

No external validation was performed; the dataset is proprietary and limited to a single center (Emergency Clinical Hospital for Plastic, Reconstructive Surgery and Burns, Bucharest), raising concerns about population and equipment bias.

The training data selection relied on 'Van Niel’s rule and physician recommendations' for approximately 37% of segments, introducing potential subjectivity and lack of standardized randomization in the training set composition.
The method was not compared against other machine learning models or deep learning architectures, limiting the assessment of the Spectral Angle Mapper's relative performance.
The study focuses only on BCC and SCC on the face/scalp/head and neck, excluding other anatomical sites and melanoma, limiting the scope of clinical relevance for broader skin cancer assessment.

The paper does not report confidence intervals for the median AUC values, only reporting median, minimum, maximum, and interquartile ranges, which limits the precision assessment of the performance metrics.
